data.title: Revisiting JEPQ: A Measured Update And Alternatives
data.description:
I am here to revisit the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ) for a few reasons. After I wrote last, there were a few comments that I thought were worth discussing. I may have been a little harsh on the Sell rating and want to revisit that. For which I will explore all use cases where JEPQ may work. I also wanted to show explicitly why there are better alternatives, so a comparison with peers (within and outside the NDX core underlying) will be presented. I will also show how my core proposition of buy and generate income from NDX works.

Performance Update

Since I last wrote, this is how JEPQ has shaped up. At the time of writing my last review, NDX was already on the way up, although it wasn't clear how far the rebound would go. As it turned out, we have had a powerful rebound. And how has JEPQ done in this strong reversal? This is not JEPQ's territory, a bull phase. So expectedly it has underperformed QQQ (a proxy for NDX) - ~9.5% versus ~16% in the index it writes options on. The last time NDX was up ~16% in August to November 2024, JEPQ had not lagged by as much. Investors who banked on that behavior to be the norm, should have had a reality check in the most recent rally. However, this is still expected behavior for a option income strategy - capped upside and I don't hold that against JEPQ. It is the lack of commensurate drawdown mitigation (as discussed in my original thesis) that sets me against JEPQ.

The Ultimate Alternative

The ultimate alternative for income seekers, I had touched upon in my earlier thesis is investing in the NDX through an ETF like QQQ (or better still QQQM for tax efficiencies) and harvest similar income as JEPQ. Why? Because through a vehicle like JEPQ, you are riding similar drawdown risks (bettered by a margin) and ultimately relying on growth of NDX to fund your income. And over a long term, the upsides are usually greater than the corrections. JEPQ by design captures a lot of the drawdown and falls short on capturing upsides.

I understand that DIY implementation of JEPQ with options is an overhead many will not be willing to sign up for. And I don't recommend that either. I am recommending a simple Buy and Hold QQQM and withdrawing income when required - lower overhead. You need income you withdraw! That's the ask. In fact, the approach allows for adjusting income withdrawals as per need and retains a possibility of keeping more on the table for capital appreciation. And believe me, as I will show later, it is the capital appreciation from the growth of the underlying that actually funds your payouts in JEPQ, not much the option layer as is the intention.

So, to withdraw income from an investment in QQQM versus the payouts you receive from JEPQ, the only factor of note is tax treatment. For FY 2024, the annual report notes only about ~$61m as qualified dividends out of a total distribution of ~$809m. That is around ~7.5% of the payouts receiving preferential tax treatment. The rest is taxed at ordinary income rates. On the contrary, a withdrawal from a lumpsum investment in QQQ is taxed at ordinary rated for withdrawals made within the first year. Beyond which more tax friendly capital gains rates apply. There is more to the tax advantages of a DIY withdrawal. Control over cashflow is one - you withdraw more or less as per requirement and don't incur forced tax consequences of a payout from JEPQ. You can also harvest tax efficiencies, offsetting withdrawals in down markets against gains.

I have replicated a similar payout as JEPQ below, on a lumpsum investment in QQQ at the start of May 2022. For a similar $100 investment in each route and similar income generated (where DIY withdrawals from QQQ is more tax efficient), I end up with a remaining capital of $130 against JEPQ's ~$109. That is a very high difference over just 3 years. And I haven't even considered a close to ~0.5% dividend payout annually from QQQ.

For those you may think, the DIY QQQ route is more volatile and should show more drawdowns, below is the NAV of both approaches compared. Yes, DIY QQQ withdrawals should in theory show wider drawdowns, but that's very little differential in favor of JEPQ when compared to the upside gains seen in QQQ.

Also, to see illustrate how the situation would have looked if NDX had not rallied as it did in the period analyzed (and that biasing my conclusions), I checked the total return behavior between July 2024 to May 2025. A period where the NDX was net flat. You will notice from the table above that despite yielding the same income, the QQQ DIY approach led to a capital invested change from ~$122 to ~$123 in the period, while the capital changed from $107 to $105 for JEPQ.

Evaluating some Use Cases and Advantages

Total returns with no significantly worse volatility profile beats most use cases in my mind. And my detailed calculations above should support why JEPQ does have better alternatives from a risk adjusted total returns perspective. But I am willing to check whether there are other use cases where JEPQ fits in.

  • Income Generation - There are Better Alternatives. The ultimate alternative being DIY withdrawals from QQQ.
  • Management Hassles - Yes. JEPQ auto pays investors. I can concede ground here. Although I am not suggesting an option DIY approach as the alternative. I am just recommending simply withdraw same as JEPQ from QQQ (or as required). If JEPQ still feels less management overheads despite tax inefficiencies and a worse total return profile, go for it.
  • Better Drawdown Management - The expectation that JEPQ should work better in a sideways or correcting market is not a myth, but overrated. In my view the option layer does more harm than good for long term investors.

To illustrate the option layer's effectiveness, I have plotted total returns of just holding the underlying versus owning JEPQ in a period between December 2024 to May 2025, where NDX was net flat. Observe how JEPQ, despite its theoretical option layer advantage, actually underperforms the underlying index. In some other flat markets, it may show the benefits of an option layer, but surely in real market conditions, the options do not offer certain structural advantage to rely upon. However, it does offer almost "certain" disadvantages in capturing upsides.

Also the drawdown management is hugely better. At the trough of the tariff sell-off, as seen in the chart above, I see 3-4 percentage point benefits at best. Not enough to make the ride less volatile than holding QQQ. And certainly not enough when we see the opportunity loss on rebounds.

As a long term investor, I should not be planning for a sideways or correction markets throughout anyway. Only in a corner use case of tactically employing JEPQ expecting such a market, does JEPQ still merit a Buy. Even then I find it hard to support a "timing the market" use case, that too diverting the objective from income (what it is designed for) versus tactical gains.

However, given the use cases of JEPQ in down markets and the drags of the alternative's management overhead, I am willing to revisit my earlier harsh Sell rating. My stance remains the same and I have outlined my concerns with the option income strategy as a general concept here. But I have been reviewing many other income ETFs since my Sell rating, and have been evaluating them within the boundaries of its known limitations. I have rated some of them as Buy because of relative performance within the option selling income strategy umbrella. As we will see in the subsequent section, JEPQ does better than a other few option income alternatives, and its rating should be in comparison to those other similar alternatives. Hence a rerating to "Hold" to be consistent with my own assessments and not penalize JEPQ out of proportion. Consider the rerating as a normalization exercise in relative grading rather than a change of stance.

Other Alternatives

First a comparison with other NDX based option strategies. I like QQQI a Buy because of its drawdown management. On those grounds, JEPQ should also make a Buy. But JEPQ has been extremely sluggish in the past tariff sell-off rebound. Hence a Hold looks a better rating - looking from a relative lens with peers.

JEPQ does far better than QYLD in total return terms, including the drawdown management bit. QYLD gains a percentage point at time in choppy markets, but JEPQ is still the better income ETF to lean into.

JEPQ also turns out better compared to QYLG and QQQY. QQQY in fact suffers from a very high yield (mostly funded through a return of capital) that leaves not enough on the table for future growth. Overall, among the peer group, JEPQ wins most of the total return battles, holds on in terms of drawdown management. But its tax efficiencies are still poorer on average, within the pack. QQQI could be a better bet overall, including tax efficiencies.

Closing Thoughts

For income investors willing to explore outside the NDX and really agnostic about where the income is derived from, I have come across a few great alternatives that can be explored. LVHI is a great choice benefiting from international diversification and delivering lower dividends, but for really lower volatility. ADX balances income with growth really well. GPIX does the same strategy but places the option layer more dynamically and ends up doing quite well (albeit on a different index - the S&P 500). I would strongly recommend RDTE, a similar strategy but on a more conducive small cap space. QDVO is the closest to JEPQ in principle and gets many things right.

Closing on the thesis, I believe income focus has many better alternatives even within an option strategy framework. My best bet is not to go there at all though. However, I will rerate JEPQ to Hold as I feel I was unusually harsh on it and wanted to convey the message of how better alternatives exist. I think that is still true, but JEPQ is definitely not the worst ETF within its peer group (NDX based option writing ETFs and income ETFs overall). Some readers did point out that JEPQ does become the outperformer consistently in a lost decade like scenario. That could be true. But you enter an income plan on equities thinking probabilities and payoffs. The probability of the markets growing in the long term are definitely higher at any given point of time. And the pay-off in a JEPQ like instrument even in a lost decade is not significant beyond a few percentage point. Sometimes, even that is missing from it's performance.

data.title: STAG Industrial: Monthly Dividend Stock To Watch
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

STAG Industrial (NYSE:STAG) is a REIT focused on owning and operating industrial properties across the United States. The stock is down in the past year as a result of the environment, but their monthly dividend combined with a potential recovery in the environment could make this an attractive opportunity.

We see significant jumps in rent in recent years, with the Q2 YTD number standing at a 26.1% cash rent increase (1.6 million of the 4.2 million square feet of leases commencing in Q2 were new leases). This is not only STAG, but a relatively common trend in the industrial segment, especially thanks to the e-commerce growth and sustained demand following the pandemic. This is actually a very relevant point to remember, because it directly impacts their future rent, meaning higher revenue and funds from operations, which should translate into higher dividends.

As I’ll mention below, this “higher dividends” point is actually a problem with STAG, but I have to note that their growth in AFFO/EPS/etc. might now have been possible without their investments, which means less money for dividend increases. They’ve been investing significant amounts of money, with 576,764 square feet (for $61.7 million) acquired YTD, with a weighted average cash capitalization rate of 6.9%, while the number of shares outstanding has been relatively flat since 2022.

The company managed to develop thanks to strategic acquisitions in the past combined with the e-commerce boom caused by the pandemic, creating unprecedented demand for the type of industrial properties STAG owns, significantly boosting the activity and collection percentages. Also, they continued to develop on a per share basis even through the pandemic and high-rate environment, which is definitely impressive.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a relatively common position for a REIT, with significant assets covering the total liabilities more than twice, but little-to-no current assets, as the company depends on the rent payments for interest/maturities. Again, this is common and is part of the business model of most REITs, with liquidity coming from rent, issuing stocks or debt or divestitures in some cases. We get $3.52 billion in equity on a $6.73 billion market cap, placing the PB at 1.91, but keep in mind that the balance sheet is backward-looking, and the real value of their properties in today’s market, when they are able to get significantly higher rent (see the double-digit increases mentioned earlier), means these assets could be worth more than what the company recorded them at back when they were acquired. If we look at it from an AFFO point of view, $3.52 billion is very little for $458.2 million generated last year, for example.

Still, there’s also the $396.8 million undrawn revolver balance if needed, as well as their committed private placement debt proceeds of $550 million (issuance announced back in April), bringing the total liquidity to an estimated $964 million. The debt maturities are also very well spread, with almost no debt left in 2025, $430 million in 2026 that will probably get refinanced and pushed into 2030+, and a weighted average interest of 4.13% on their debt, placing them in a good financial position.

Dividends & Buybacks

The dividend stands at ~4.15% today, which is among the company’s lowest in the past decade. This represents a ~60% payout ratio on their FFO, so there is potential to see them increase it if they want to.

However, that’s a big if. Now, I can understand limiting the dividend increases post-pandemic for some years, since the environment was very favorable for the industry overall and it’s better to be safe and capitalize on it than sorry, but the company was terrible at increasing its dividend even before that. 0.68% - 0.69% CAGR is far from beating inflation, and this yield is not really attractive in the current market compared to many other peers, as I’ll mention below. But again, they’re investing significantly in their growth and preparing for the future, so minimal dividend increases may be just a matter of time, until the management considers higher rates should suit them.

To be fair, as I’ll mention below, we are already in a time where the theoretically risk-free US treasuries pay better yields than many REITs, so there’s not that much of a reason for them to “fight” that to attract shareholders if they’re very long-term oriented. The fact that they also didn’t issue that many shares during this time also kind of reinforces this idea for me, since I believe they’re most likely expecting rate cuts to result in better share prices as well, leading to a switch from investing using cash to investing through issuing highly valued stock instead.

Risks & Opportunities

As a REIT - especially one in the industrial sector - probably their highest potential near-term catalyst will be potential rate cuts. Not only will it help the company directly by reducing the cost of debt (either if they refinance or directly lower their variable interest payments) - with interest payments currently at a record $120.3 million in the past 12 months - but it would also help their clients. This way, it will be easier for companies to invest, while rate cuts would also boost the economy, improve spending, and the overall economic activity.

This reduction in interest will also result in higher funds available, allowing STAG to increase their dividend, potentially (and hopefully) at better rates than before.

Not only that, but that should also be beneficial for dividend stocks in particular, since the market would be required to risk up if it wants good yields. We’ve had US treasuries (which are considered the risk-free assets) trading at ~4-5% yields for so long, which should theoretically have a negative effect on the demand for dividend stocks, as money would chase the better risk-adjusted yields. This is why most dividend stocks increased back when the Fed started cutting, and some are now reverting due to the tariffs causing fear and uncertainty around inflation and the future of rate cuts. I don’t believe we’ll get back to a zero-rate environment any time soon, but a reduction in rates is nonetheless beneficial.

In the near future, I believe we should see rate cuts come, even if Powell stops them entirely (recently announced another pause as the economy’s growth slowed down, with no hint of a September cut). That’s because his term ends in May 2026, and President Trump will nominate a new chair for the Fed during that time. This time, I believe we could see him choose a person that would likely cut rates regardless of the economy, especially since Trump has been very vocal about the Fed needing to cut rates lately, despite saying the cuts from a year ago were a bad idea.

Still, even without rate cuts, we can see significant improvements for STAG already, with the company recently increasing their Core FFO outlook for this year to $2.48 - $2.52, as leasing momentum accelerates and development initiatives advance, with the CEO noting that the first half’s results exceeded their initial business plan. It’s impressive that the company has addressed 90.8% of the expected leased square feet already this year, with cash leasing spreads expected to be in the 24.5% area. Plus, the developments are advancing, with a new 95-5 joint venture for a 500,000 square foot warehouse that should come in Q2 2026 with a targeted 7.1% cash yield, adding to their future FFO.

Peer Comparison

Looking at their peers, we see quite an interesting industry, with Prologis (PLD) being by far the leader, with a more than 15 times higher market cap than STAG and the rest, while there are many players similar or smaller than STAG, such as First Industrial Realty Trust (FR), Terreno Realty Corporation (TRNO), EastGroup Properties (EGP), Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), Americold Realty Trust (COLD) and LXP Industrial Trust (LXP), focusing either on secondary markets or only a small number of cities/states.

Although there’s no news or clear plans about this, I believe it’s not impossible to see PLD continue to buy competitors (STAG included) as they did in recent years, since they need very significant investments to move the needle because they are a more than $100 billion market cap company. Still, they seem to be focused on their international expansion now, but I wouldn’t count out the possibility of seeing STAG enter a merger or acquisition in the future.

On the dividend side, we see STAG somewhere in the middle of the industry when it comes to the yield, being the best in terms of consecutive years of growth and payout ratio and the only one paying monthly dividends, but the worst in terms of actual dividend growth.

Valuation

For the valuation, I’ll adjust last year’s AFFO a similar 4.17% growth rate as the Core FFO (their midpoint expected level), leading to $477.3 million in 2025, growing this at a CAGR of 4% in the next 5 years followed by 3.25% in the following 5, and a terminal growth rate of 2.5%. Assuming a WACC of 8% (in line with market estimates) and considering $3.04 billion in net debt plus 186.62 million shares outstanding, the resulting model yields an enterprise value of ~$9.37 billion. After subtracting net debt, this leads to an equity value of ~$6.33 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of about $33.93.

This is almost exactly the current level, although the WACC will remain a key factor in this. As rates get cut, we could also see this go to levels such as 7% in the near term (I’m assuming rates will not go back to zero soon), which can lead to prices in the $45 range, similar to what they were at a few years ago.

Conclusions

Overall, I believe STAG is a Hold now, even if there is potential to see the stock go up once rates are cut - same as with any other dividend stock - but I think there are better dividend stocks out there. The company is healthy, developing in a key industry for the future, but the dividend yield is not that attractive for a REIT, and I don’t think growing it at a faster rate would change this. Still, I believe that once rate cuts come, there is potential to see them switch to a higher focus on funding their expansion through low-cost debt and issuing highly valued stock instead of cash, allowing them to hike the dividend at better rates than before.

data.title: Guardant Health, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Guardant Health, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Franklin U.S. Focused Growth SMA Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equities collectively rose during 2025’s second quarter (2Q25) after bouncing back from their April lows. Nine out of the 11 global equity sectors advanced; information technology (IT), communication services and industrials produced the highest returns, while energy and health care retreated. In terms of investment style, global growth stocks significantly outperformed their value counterparts, regardless of company size. US President Donald Trump’s sweeping “reciprocal” tariffs sparked market volatility, but easing trade tensions and delayed tariff hikes reduced recession fears and supported consumer sentiment despite elevated inflation in some parts of the world, amid minimal market impacts from the Israel-Iran conflict and oil price fluctuations.
  • Contributors: Favorable stock selectivity versus the benchmark index, especially in the IT and industrials sectors. To a lesser extent, a strategic lack of exposure to the underperforming energy, materials and real estate sectors also boosted relative returns.
  • Detractors: Detrimental overweightings in consumer staples distribution and retail as well as life sciences tools and services; a lack of exposure to utilities companies; and roughly 2% of uninvested assets sidelined in cash.
  • Outlook: We remain optimistic about the resilience of the US economy even in this environment of policy uncertainty. Technology, in general—and artificial intelligence (AI), robotics and hyperscale cloud computing, in particular—is inherently disinflationary and a large enough force to affect the entire economy through increased productivity.

Performance Review

  • Despite elevated portfolio volatility throughout 2Q25, strong stock selection—particularly in the IT, industrials, consumer discretionary and communication services sectors—helped the strategy outperform the benchmark Russell 1000 Growth Index by a wide margin in an up market.
  • Our biggest sector exposure over the past several years has been IT, and 2Q25 was no different as IT covered 44.4% of the portfolio (on average). This has served us well, as nothing has come close to the performance of IT companies since the strategy’s inception in 2020. However, over the April–June span, we maintained a 3.0 percentage-point underweighting in IT and trimmed some positions because innovation is spreading beyond IT into the entire economy. While this level of exposure slightly pressured relative returns, it had little impact on the much larger positive influence of stock selection.
  • Within IT, having no exposure to Apple (AAPL) gave us a major upside boost in relative terms, as the company shed just over 7% of its equity value. We also saw exceptional rallies in overweighted or off-index IT holdings in various industries, led by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Broadcom (AVGO) and NVIDIA in semiconductors and semiconductor equipment; Cloudflare (NET) in IT services; and ServiceNow (NOW) and AppLovin in software.
  • Outside the IT sector, notable contributors included heavily overweighted stakes in Axon Enterprise (AXON) in the aerospace and defense industry, commission-free trading platform Robinhood Markets (HOOD) (purchased during the period) in capital markets, and Meta Platforms in interactive media and services; along with rallying consumer discretionary sector positions in Latin American e-commerce and fintech giant MercadoLibre (MELI) (off-benchmark) in broadline retail, and food delivery and logistics platform provider DoorDash (DASH) (overweight). In particular, Robinhood’s share price more than doubled due to strong financial performance (including record net deposits and revenue growth), expansion into cryptocurrencies with tokenized trading and a new Layer 2 blockchain, and bullish analyst upgrades driven by increased trading volumes.
  • Given the magnitude of the strategy’s 2Q25 results, the detractors were comparatively mild. In the financials sector, an overweight position in Mastercard (MA) (averaging 4.5% of total net assets) increased mildly and captured only a fraction of the benchmark index’s overall gain, while a comparatively smaller investment in Tradeweb Markets (TW) depreciated slightly. In a similar vein, the gain for our solitary position in the consumer staples sector—Costco Wholesale (COST)—was far behind the index.
  • Elsewhere in the portfolio, relative outperformance in the IT sector was trimmed by a slightly below-benchmark exposure to Microsoft (MSFT) as its share price rose more than 30% in 2Q25. Software industry results were further compromised by keeping about eight times the index’s weighting in HubSpot (HUBS) as its shares declined modestly, and by our lack of investments in several high-flying index component companies like Oracle (ORCL), Palantir Technologies (PLTR) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) Holdings. In health care, off-benchmark Argenx sold off in the biotechnology industry, as did Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) in life sciences tools and services, while medical robotics specialist Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) posted lagging gains versus the index.

Outlook

  • We are hopeful and enthusiastic about the potential and current productivity gains from AI, as its use cases continue to develop. Large language models (LLMs) are advancing at a fast pace, with the first returns on investment starting to coalesce, notably in the digital advertising space. Use cases are also being proven elsewhere, including in e-commerce, finance and health care. Additionally, AI developers are increasingly focused on achieving agentic AI. Areas where agentic AI is quickly taking shape include software development and deep research.
  • We are tracking other bright spots for innovation in the economy beyond AI, including aerospace and defense, cryptocurrency, robotics, genomics and green energy. Regarding aerospace and defense, a new class of companies are challenging the primacy of large, established defense contractors. These nimble companies are developing new technologies, including autonomous drones and sophisticated military software. The crypto space is also maturing, with relevant applications in smart contracts and payment systems coming to the fore. The use cases for robotics are also accelerating as AI improves the software behind the hardware. Genomics is still early in its development, with scientists and managers addressing the complexities of the medicine to achieve US Food & Drug Administration approval. Finally, green energy initiatives continue to advance worldwide, with the cost curve—particularly for solar energy—coming down and with new ideas around nuclear and geothermal energy grabbing attention.

Top Equity Issuers (% of Total)

MICROSOFT CORP 10.37

*Cumulative total returns

Benchmark(s)

Benchmark = Russell 1000 Growth Index

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. An investment in this strategy can lose value. Please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights for the latest performance. Investors cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges.

Fees: Periods less than one year are not annualized. Performance results are for the composite which includes all actual, fully discretionary accounts with substantially similar investment policies and objectives managed to the composite’s investment strategy. Composite returns are stated in U.S. dollars and assume reinvestment of any dividends, interest income, capital gains, or other earnings. The composite may include account(s) that are gross of fees and pure gross of fees. “Pure” gross-of-fee returns do not reflect the deduction of any expenses, including transaction costs. A traditional (or “true”) gross-of-fee return reflects performance after the reduction of transaction costs but before the reduction of the investment advisory fee. The gross-of-fee return may include a blend of “true” gross-of-fee returns for non-wrap accounts and “pure” gross-of-fee returns for wrap accounts. Net-of-fee returns is reduced by a model “wrap fee” (3.0% is the maximum anticipated wrap fee for equity and balanced portfolios) which includes trading expenses as well as investment management, administrative and custodial fees. The model wrap fee used represents the highest anticipated wrap fee applicable to the strategy. Actual fees and account minimums may vary.

For fee schedules, contact your financial professional, or if you enter into an agreement directly with Franklin Templeton Private Portfolio Group, LLC (“FTPPG”), refer to FTPPG’s Form ADV Part 2A disclosure document. Management and performance of individual accounts may vary for reasons that include the existence of different implementation practices and model requirements in different investment programs.

To obtain specific information on available products and services or a GIPS Report, contact your Franklin Templeton separately managed account sales team at (800) DIAL BEN/342-5236.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. To the extent the portfolio invests in a concentration of certain securities, regions or industries, it is subject to increased volatility. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. The investment style may become out of favor, which may have a negative impact on performance. Active management does not ensure gains or protect against market declines. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or portfolio. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the portfolio’s selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

These materials are being provided for illustrative and informational purposes only. The information contained herein is obtained from multiple sources that are believed to be reliable. However, such information has not been verified, and may be different from the information included in documents and materials created by the sponsor firm in whose investment program a client participates. Some sponsor firms may require that these materials be preceded or accompanied by investment profiles or other documents or materials prepared by such sponsor firms, which will be provided upon a client’s request. For additional information, documents and/or materials, please speak to your Financial Professional or contact your sponsor firm.

Franklin Templeton (FT) is not undertaking to provide impartial advice. Nothing herein is intended to provide fiduciary advice. FT has a financial interest.

The Russell 1000 Growth Index measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE.

Important data provider notices and terms available at Third Party Content Providers and Disclosures. All data is subject to change.

Separately Managed Accounts (SMAS) are investment services provided by Franklin Templeton Private Portfolio Group, LLC (FTPPG), a federally registered investment advisor. Management is implemented by FTPPG, the designated subadvisor or, in the case of certain programs, the program sponsor or its designee.

data.description: The following slide deck was published by Aurora Innovation, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: ASML: Seems Overvalued And Faces Growing Structural Risks
data.description:
ASML performed fairly well in Q2. Despite this, we believe the stock is overvalued and faces growing structural risks that the market is underestimating. ASML remains a SELL for us right now.

Despite ASML’s (NASDAQ:ASML) good performance in Q2 and an upbeat near-term outlook, we believe the stock is overvalued and faces growing structural risks that the market is underestimating. The slowdown of bookings, rising inventories, and geopolitical instability threaten the company’s ability to greatly improve its performance in the future. Although ASML remains a key part of the global semiconductor supply chain, its current premium valuation offers little room for further growth. While ASML’s shares have already declined by over 10% since our latest coverage on the company in June, we believe that the bottom has not been reached yet, and we retain our SELL rating for its stock for now.

The Future Growth Is Not Guaranteed

A couple of weeks ago, ASML posted a relatively good earnings report for Q2, which showed that the company made €7.7 billion in net sales while its gross margin was 53.7%. In addition to that, orders rose 41% sequentially, while the EUV revenue is expected to grow by around 30% Y/Y in fiscal 2025. However, those are the only positive factors in the recent report, in our opinion. We should not ignore the fact that the management decided not to confirm growth for the next year by saying the following:

Against this backdrop, while we are still paying for growth in 2026, we cannot confirm it at this stage.

This might suggest that there are issues with new orders due to the rising macroeconomic and geopolitical challenges, coupled with customer-specific capital expenditure delays. This is particularly concerning given that ASML shares are still trading at a 7% premium to their historical average forward P/E of 25.2x.

Also, while ASML highlighted strong EUV demand driven by the increasing need for AI infrastructure, actual unit growth is slowing down. In Q2, the EUV tools generated only €2.7 billion in sales, and the unit growth has slowed as customers are buying less of the higher-performance NXE:3800E systems. This means that even at higher ASPs, the revenue advantage from new shipments is slowly disappearing. Even more concerning is the fact that the net order book trend has remained flat for almost two years. The latest order-to-bill ratio of 0.79x is at a 5-year low, which might suggest that sales are now largely driven by legacy orders instead of new orders.

When it comes to geopolitical risks, they are also rising. In Q2, China accounted for more than 25% of ASML’s revenues, largely driven by Deep UV tools. While this helped offset declines in other areas, the recent €1.4 billion adjustment for Chinese withdrawals shows that the demand in the country for the company’s products remains greatly volatile.

As for the American market, under the new US-EU trade deal, European goods will be tariffed at 15%, which is still a lot considering that it was not the case even half a year ago. That’s why it’s not a surprise that during the latest conference call, the management said the following when discussing the impact of tariffs:

The indirect impact is complex and very difficult to determine.

All of this may lead to the customers delaying orders and waiting for better times, especially considering that ASML’s systems cost up to $400 million and require 12-18 months of lead time. That is why the lack of a revenue forecast for next year and uncertainty about the future are making ASML shares a risky investment at this point, since the future growth is not guaranteed.

The Intrinsic Value of ASML

We have updated our ASML’s valuation model and included the most accurate and up-to-date data. The perpetual growth rate in our updated model is 3%, which we use all the time when valuing semiconductor businesses. The model was created when ASML was trading at $711.25 per share. The balance sheet data has been taken from the latest earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 8.44%. We calculated it by figuring out ASML’s cost of equity and cost of debt. To calculate the cost of debt, we used its TTM numbers. To calculate the cost of equity, we used the CAPM model, using a risk-free rate is 4.38%, a beta of 1.22, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

For forecasting ASML’s growth, our sales and EBIT assumptions are based on the company’s guidance and market analysts' estimates for the next 5 years. ASML’s management has guided for approximately 15% growth in revenue for FY2025. The model incorporates a 17% tax rate, consistent with the management expectations for FY2025. All other assumptions remain largely in line with ASML’s historical performance.

Based on these assumptions, our model estimates ASML’s enterprise value to be $197.19 billion. To determine the equity value, we added the company’s cash and cash equivalents to the enterprise value and subtracted outstanding total debt, arriving at an estimated equity value of $201.33 billion. After that, we divided the company’s equity value by the number of its outstanding shares and figured out that ASML’s intrinsic value is $518.38 per share, which is around 27.12% below the current market price. Considering the valuation and all the risks described above, we decided to stick with our SELL rating for ASML.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

While we remain pessimistic about ASML’s ability to significantly improve its performance in the future, several factors could undermine our bearish thesis.

First, the demand for advanced lithography systems could prove more resilient than expected, especially if the adoption of High-NA EUV accelerates in the following years. After all, customers such as TSMC and Intel have publicly committed to using advanced nodes, and any faster-than-expected ramp-up of 2nm or below could lead to stronger orders.

Secondly, the geopolitical situation might improve in the upcoming months. If tensions between the United States and China ease, ASML could maintain its DVU tool sales to China, which could help support its order book and revenue in 2026. In addition, new regional fabs in India or Southeast Asia could open up additional demand.

Finally, ASML’s technical moat remains extremely strong. The company has a near monopoly in EUV technology and a tightly integrated supply chain that makes disruptions difficult even for well-funded competitors. If execution remains solid and cyclical demand recovers sooner than expected, the stock could resume gains despite the current difficulties.

Final Thoughts

ASML’s strategic position in the chip supply chain is undeniable. But that doesn’t shield it from cyclical realities, slowing customer demand and worsening macroeconomic environment. We believe that unless ASML grows better-than-expected in the second half of the year, its stock price is headed lower. That is why ASML remains a SELL for us.

data.title: BABX Can Provide China Exposure During Trade Talks
data.description:
The GraniteShares 2x Long BABA Daily ETF (BABX) is a leveraged strategy designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of Alibaba Group Holdings Limited (BABA). Given the dynamics of leveraged funds, BABX should only be used for daily performance and should not be considered as a long-term holding.

BABX creates the intended exposure to BABA through the use of swap derivatives in order to achieve the targeted 2x daily performance of BABA. Roughly 200% of the portfolio holdings are in BABA swap positions. Within the strategy, BABX is the payer in which BABX receives daily performance exposure to BABA shares while paying a fixed rate. The swap positions are settled at the end of every trading day, resulting in the performance of the swap to reset daily. This means that the returns on the swap positions will be settled on a day-to-day basis. This is reflected in the performance of BABX, in which performance will be reset daily.

This can be visualized by the relative performance since the inception of BABX. Despite BABA returning 19%, BABX has returned -14.76%.

Because BABX has a daily reset, returns can be amplified over longer holding periods, resulting in a compounding effect that can significantly benefit the investor or deteriorate in value. This can exceptionally be seen during multiple days of upward or downward returns in which the compounding effect sets in.

For example, during periods of positive returns on BABA, BABX can exhibit substantial gains over the underlying stock.

The same can be true to the downside, as multiple days of consecutive losses can result in significant value deterioration and, in some cases, may not be recoverable.

Daily performance can be impacted by multiple variables, including swap performance, trading activity on the ETF, and volatility throughout the trading day. High levels of volatility can create some challenges for the ETF to meet the 2x daily performance target and may result in some dispersion between BABA share performance and the performance of BABX. Though the strategy provides around 200% exposure to BABA, the 2x daily performance target is not guaranteed and may show some dispersions depending on these factors.

BABX exhibits some liquidity challenges as the assets under management are relatively low at $135mm. Daily volumes should be adequate for active trading strategies with an average of 825k shares trading hands on a daily basis.

Relative to peer single name leveraged ETFs, BABX may exhibit relatively low liquidity that may impact trading activity during periods of lower trading volumes. Compared to peer strategy KraneShares 2x Long BABA Daily ETF (KBAB), BABX may be a more optimal trading vehicle given its depth despite the higher fee.

Risks Related To Leveraged Strategies

There are inherent risks in investing in leveraged strategies. Because these strategies are meant to achieve their stated multiple over the underlying index on a daily basis, holding these strategies beyond a single day can lead to skewed performance, especially following consecutive days of upside or downside risk. This can lead to a compounding effect as well as value decay as a result of the daily reset in the derivatives positions.

Leveraged strategies also add an extra layer of risk to amplify downside potential. For example, if the underlying index were to appreciate 2% in a single day, a 3x strategy would appreciate by 6%, meaning that an investment of $100 would increase to $106. If the following day results in a -2% decline in the underlying index, or a -6% decline in the leveraged strategy, the investment will decline to $99.64.

Holding leveraged strategies for longer than a single day can lead to a compounding effect on one’s position, potentially leading to significant skews from the intended multiplier. During multiple days of downside risk, an investor can experience significant value decay, potentially resulting in the inability to recover losses.

Liquidity must be considered prior to investing in a leveraged fund strategy. Given that these funds are designed to be traded daily, trading volumes must be considered to ensure that cost-in/cost-out isn’t deteriorated by spreads.

Traders must also consider additional taxes on short-term capital gains as well as trading fees prior to considering a leveraged trading strategy.

You can review additional risks here.

Before making a trading or investment decision in leveraged strategies, be sure to fully understand your risk profile and set a trading mandate to manage these risks. I believe preemptively setting parameters for selling out of a position is prudent in ensuring that an investor appropriately manages downside risk.

Final Thoughts

BABX may provide trades with an appealing vehicle for amplifying daily returns over BABA. Traders should be aware of the potential liquidity risks BABX poses, particularly with the relatively low AUM within the strategy. Given the risk of value decay over longer holding periods, I cannot recommend investors allocate to BABX as an investment position. I recommend BABX with a HOLD rating.

BABX could potentially be an appealing strategy to play the US & China trade talks. Accordingly, both sides are looking to extend the trade truce beyond the August deadline to iron out all the details for an agreement. A positive outcome could potentially lead to positive trading activity for BABX.

data.title: Franklin Equity Income Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • JPMorgan Chase (JPM) was a leading contributor to financials sector performance. The recovery in equity markets led to better-than-expected revenues in the first quarter of 2025, for the financial services giant. Its revenues were helped by higher asset management and investment banking fees and strong trading results.
  • In the IT sector, shares of software developer and chipmaker Broadcom (AVGO) moved higher following its release of strong fiscal second-quarter 2025 financial results. Demand for artificial intelligence-related semiconductors remains quite strong, and we believe the company’s outlook through its fiscal year 2025 and into its fiscal year 2026 continues to look promising.
  • In the health care sector, shares of UnitedHealth Group (UNH) declined in the face of several negative headlines about the health insurer. Among them were the resignation of the company’s chief executive officer, the company’s decision to withdraw its 2025 outlook and the ongoing investigation by the US Department of Justice into its billing practices. We reduced our exposure significantly over the period.

Outlook

  • We have a positive outlook for corporate earnings and the financial markets overall. The recent passing of significant legislation in Congress should support economic growth, and the prospects for infrastructure spending, lower regulation and ongoing productivity gains from technology should also be supportive. While the news about US tariffs on key trade partners in April was more impactful than we anticipated, we believe trade agreements are likely to be reached with key partners, which can allow for a more positive macroeconomic environment to reemerge. We believe this can support broader market participation across industries while allowing key IT companies to continue to do well.
  • We expect the strategy to hold up relatively well during this period of higher volatility given its lower predicted beta and risk profile. We believe that the powerful secular themes seen throughout the strategy, like infrastructure improvement, energy transition and grid hardening, should continue to be positive areas of investment as they can provide support for companies during times of economic uncertainty as those themes play out over time.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 5/15/2008. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (a) for Advisor Class periods prior to 5/15/2008, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 02/28/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Distributions are not guaranteed and are subject to change. Investments in equity-linked notes often have risks similar to their underlying securities, which could include management risk, market risk and, as applicable, foreign securities and currency risks. Convertible securities are subject to the risks of stocks when the underlying stock price is high relative to the conversion price and debt securities when the underlying stock price is low relative to the conversion price. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) is an unmanaged index composed of 30 blue-chip stocks, each with annual sales exceeding $7 billion. The DJIA is price-weighted, reflects large-cap companies representative of U.S. industry, and historically has moved in tandem with other major market indexes such as the S&P 500. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

Nasdaq Composite Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to represent the performance of NASDAQ securities and includes over 3,000 stocks. Source: Nasdaq OMX.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved. The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 06/30/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund’s monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund’s Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 3 and 4 and fund’s Advisor Class shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 3 and 4 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund’s Morningstar Rating™ for Class A and Advisor Class shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings.

Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

© Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.
